serving.natural_language_portion: A single fried egg, typically served as an additional side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, fried
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data for 'Egg, whole, cooked, fried' (FDC ID 172128) from USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index for fried egg is estimated as low (set to 1) due to its negligible carbohydrate content, consistent with general egg data in the University of Sydney GI Database where eggs are typically not assigned a specific GI value due to their minimal glycemic impact.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of added salt during preparation, as eggs themselves are naturally low in sodium at approximately 196 mg per 100 g when fried.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried eggs have a very low glycemic impact due to their minimal carbohydrate content of approximately 1.1 g per 100 g, making them a suitable food choice for managing blood sugar levels in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While eggs contain approximately 398 mg cholesterol per 100 g, recent evidence suggests dietary cholesterol has less impact on blood cholesterol than saturated and trans fats; moderation is advised for those with cardiovascular disease, particularly regarding cooking oil choices.

llm_training.natural_language_description: An 'Add Egg' (加煎蛋) typically refers to a fried egg served as an additional side dish or topping in various Asian cuisines, including Thai. It is prepared by frying a whole egg in oil until the whites are set and the yolk is cooked to the desired consistency, ranging from runny to firm. The taste is savory and rich, with a slightly crispy edge from frying. Nutritionally, a fried egg is an excellent source of high-quality protein (13.6 g per 100 g), healthy fats, and essential vitamins and minerals including Vitamin D (2.1 mcg), Vitamin B12 (0.89 mcg), selenium (29.8 mcg), and Vitamin A (149 mcg RAE). Its minimal carbohydrate content of approximately 1.1 g per 100 g makes it a food with a very low glycemic impact, suitable for blood sugar management in Type 2 Diabetes. It adds a satisfying texture and nutritional boost to many meals such as Pad Thai, curries, and rice dishes.
common_user_queries: Is fried egg good for diabetes?
How to cook a fried egg?

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is an exact match from USDA SR Legacy for 'Egg, whole, cooked, fried' (FDC ID 172128). GI is consistently estimated as low for eggs due to negligible carbohydrate content, supported by the University of Sydney GI Database's general approach to such foods.
notes: Dietary fibre_g is naturally zero as eggs contain no dietary fibre per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is naturally zero as eggs contain no ascorbic acid per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Glycemic index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains minimal glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172128.
